You are an LLVM compiler expert specializing in test case generation and bug detection. You are conducting LLVM code review now.

## Context
Maintainers provided a patch, but patches often have edge cases or introduce new issues.

## Your Task
You will work in two phases:
- Phase 1: Analyze the fix to identify potential issues, gaps, and edge cases. Propose test strategies.
- Phase 2: Generate targeted test cases by modifying existing tests to target identified issues

## You Will Receive
- Original patch description and related test case(s)
- The code region that was modified (the patch)
- Information about the optimization pass and component involved

## Tools You Can Use

You have multiple tools for each phase, but you should try to avoid using them with same inputs repeatedly to reduce redundant calls.

**CRITICAL**: You MUST use tool call every action in Phase 1 and Phase 2.

1654 .. _nofpclass:
1655 
1656 ``nofpclass(<test mask>)``
1657     This attribute applies to parameters and return values with
1658     floating-point and vector of floating-point types, as well as
1659     :ref:`supported aggregates <fastmath_return_types>` of such types
1660     (matching the supported types for :ref:`fast-math flags <fastmath>`).
1661     The test mask has the same format as the second argument to the
1662     :ref:`llvm.is.fpclass <llvm.is.fpclass>`, and indicates which classes
1663     of floating-point values are not permitted for the value. For example,
1664     a bitmask of 3 indicates the parameter may not be a NaN.
1665 
1666     If the value is a floating-point class indicated by the
1667     ``nofpclass`` test mask, a :ref:`poison value <poisonvalues>` is
1668     passed or returned instead.
1669 
1670 .. code-block:: text
1671   :caption: The following invariants hold
1672 
1673        @llvm.is.fpclass(nofpclass(test_mask) %x, test_mask) => false
1674        @llvm.is.fpclass(nofpclass(test_mask) %x, ~test_mask) => true
1675        nofpclass(all) => poison
1676 ..
1677 
1678    In textual IR, various string names are supported for readability
1679    and can be combined. For example ``nofpclass(nan pinf nzero)``
1680    evaluates to a mask of 547.
1681 
1682    This does not depend on the floating-point environment. For
1683    example, a function parameter marked ``nofpclass(zero)`` indicates
1684    no zero inputs. If this is applied to an argument in a function
1685    marked with :ref:`denormal_fpenv <denormal_fpenv>`
1686    indicating zero treatment of input denormals, it does not imply the
1687    value cannot be a denormal value which would compare equal to 0.
1688

1692     | Name  | floating-point class | Bitmask value |

1727 
1728 ``alignstack(<n>)``
1729     This indicates the alignment that should be considered by the backend when
1730     assigning this parameter or return value to a stack slot during calling
1731     convention lowering. The enforcement of the specified alignment is
1732     target-dependent, as target-specific calling convention rules may override
1733     this value. This attribute serves the purpose of carrying language-specific
1734     alignment information that is not mapped to base types in the backend (for
1735     example, over-alignment specification through language attributes).
1736 
1737 ``allocalign``
1738     The function parameter marked with this attribute is the alignment in bytes of the
1739     newly allocated block returned by this function. The returned value must either have
1740     the specified alignment or be the null pointer. The return value MAY be more aligned
1741     than the requested alignment, but not less aligned.  Invalid (e.g., non-power-of-2)
1742     alignments are permitted for the allocalign parameter, so long as the returned pointer
1743     is null. This attribute may only be applied to integer parameters.
1744 
1745 ``allocptr``
1746     The function parameter marked with this attribute is the pointer
1747     that will be manipulated by the allocator. For a realloc-like
1748     function the pointer will be invalidated upon success (but the
1749     same address may be returned), for a free-like function the
1750     pointer will always be invalidated.
1751 
1752 ``readnone``
1753     This attribute indicates that the function does not dereference that
1754     pointer argument, even though it may read or write the memory that the
1755     pointer points to if accessed through other pointers.
1756 
1757     If a function reads from or writes to a readnone pointer argument, the
1758     behavior is undefined.
1759 
1760 ``readonly``
1761     This attribute indicates that the function does not write through this
1762     pointer argument, even though it may write to the memory that the pointer
1763     points to.
1764 
1765     If a function writes to a readonly pointer argument, the behavior is
1766     undefined.
1767 
1768 ``writeonly``
1769     This attribute indicates that the function may write to, but does not read
1770     through this pointer argument (even though it may read from the memory that
1771     the pointer points to).
1772 
1773     This attribute is understood in the same way as the ``memory(write)``
1774     attribute. That is, the pointer may still be read as long as the read is
1775     not observable outside the function. See the ``memory`` documentation for
1776     precise semantics.
1777 
1778 ``writable``
1779     This attribute is only meaningful in conjunction with ``dereferenceable(N)``
1780     or another attribute that implies the first ``N`` bytes of the pointer
1781     argument are dereferenceable.
1782 
1783     In that case, the attribute indicates that the first ``N`` bytes will be
1784     (non-atomically) loaded and stored back on entry to the function.
1785 
1786     This implies that it's possible to introduce spurious stores on entry to
1787     the function without introducing traps or data races. This does not
1788     necessarily hold throughout the whole function, as the pointer may escape
1789     to a different thread during the execution of the function. See also the
1790     :ref:`atomic optimization guide <Optimization outside atomic>`
1791 
1792     The "other attributes" that imply dereferenceability are
1793     ``dereferenceable_or_null`` (if the pointer is non-null) and the
1794     ``sret``, ``byval``, ``byref``, ``inalloca``, ``preallocated`` family of
1795     attributes. Note that not all of these combinations are useful, e.g.
1796     ``byval`` arguments are known to be writable even without this attribute.
1797 
1798     The ``writable`` attribute cannot be combined with ``readnone``,
1799     ``readonly`` or a ``memory`` attribute that does not contain
1800     ``argmem: write``.
1801 
1802 ``initializes((Lo1, Hi1), ...)``
1803     This attribute indicates that the function initializes the ranges of the
1804     pointer parameter's memory ``[%p+LoN, %p+HiN)``. Colloquially, this means
1805     that all bytes in the specified range are written before the function
1806     returns, and not read prior to the initializing write. If the function
1807     unwinds, the write may not happen.
1808 
1809     Formally, this is specified in terms of an "initialized" shadow state for
1810     all bytes in the range, which is set to "not initialized" at function entry.
1811     If a memory access is performed through a pointer based on the argument,
1812     and an accessed byte has not been marked as "initialized" yet, then:
1813 
1814      * If the byte is stored with a non-volatile, non-atomic write, mark it as
1815        "initialized".
1816      * If the byte is stored with a volatile or atomic write, the behavior is
1817        undefined.
1818      * If the byte is loaded, return a poison value.
1819 
1820     Additionally, if the function returns normally, write an undef value to all
1821     bytes that are part of the range and have not been marked as "initialized".
1822 
1823     This attribute only holds for the memory accessed via this pointer
1824     parameter. Other arbitrary accesses to the same memory via other pointers
1825     are allowed.
1826 
1827     The ``writable`` or ``dereferenceable`` attribute do not imply the
1828     ``initializes`` attribute. The ``initializes`` attribute does not imply
1829     ``writeonly`` since ``initializes`` allows reading from the pointer
1830     after writing.
1831 
1832     This attribute is a list of constant ranges in ascending order with no
1833     overlapping or consecutive list elements. ``LoN/HiN`` are 64-bit integers,
1834     and negative values are allowed in case the argument points partway into
1835     an allocation. An empty list is not allowed.
1836 
1837     On a ``byval`` argument, ``initializes`` refers to the given parts of the
1838     callee copy being overwritten. A ``byval`` callee can never initialize the
1839     original caller memory passed to the ``byval`` argument.
1840 
1841 ``dead_on_unwind``
1842     At a high level, this attribute indicates that the pointer argument is dead
1843     if the call unwinds, in the sense that the caller will not depend on the
1844     contents of the memory. Stores that would only be visible on the unwind
1845     path can be elided.
1846 
1847     More precisely, the behavior is as-if any memory written through the
1848     pointer during the execution of the function is overwritten with a poison
1849     value on unwind. This includes memory written by the implicit write implied
1850     by the ``writable`` attribute. The caller is allowed to access the affected
1851     memory, but all loads that are not preceded by a store will return poison.
1852 
1853     This attribute cannot be applied to return values.
1854 
1855 ``dead_on_return`` or ``dead_on_return(<n>)``
1856     This attribute indicates that the memory pointed to by the argument is dead
1857     upon function return, both upon normal return and if the calls unwinds, meaning
1858     that the caller will not depend on its contents. Stores that would be observable
1859     either on the return path or on the unwind path may be elided. A number of
1860     bytes known to be dead may optionally be provided in parentheses. If a number
1861     of bytes is not specified, all memory reachable through the pointer is marked as
1862     dead on return.
1863 
1864     Specifically, the behavior is as-if any memory written through the pointer
1865     during the execution of the function is overwritten with a poison value
1866     upon function return. The caller may access the memory, but any load
1867     not preceded by a store will return poison. If a byte count is specified,
1868     only writes within the specified range are overwritten with poison on
1869     function return.
1870 
1871     This attribute does not imply aliasing properties. For pointer arguments that
1872     do not alias other memory locations, ``noalias`` attribute may be used in
1873     conjunction. Conversely, this attribute always implies ``dead_on_unwind``. When
1874     a byte count is specified, ``dead_on_unwind`` is implied only for that range.
1875 
1876     This attribute cannot be applied to return values.
1877 
1878 ``range(<ty> <a>, <b>)``
1879     This attribute expresses the possible range of the parameter or return value.
1880     If the value is not in the specified range, it is converted to poison.
1881     The arguments passed to ``range`` have the following properties:
1882 
1883     -  The type must match the scalar type of the parameter or return value.
1884     -  The pair ``a,b`` represents the range ``[a,b)``.
1885     -  Both ``a`` and ``b`` are constants.
1886     -  The range is allowed to wrap.
1887     -  The empty range is represented using ``0,0``.
1888     -  Otherwise, ``a`` and ``b`` are not allowed to be equal.
1889 
1890     This attribute may only be applied to parameters or return values with integer
1891     or vector of integer types.
1892 
1893     For vector-typed parameters, the range is applied element-wise.

arguments:
{"thoughts":"Fix Understanding:
The patch adds a new function `addNoFPClassAttrsTopDown` to the `FunctionAttrs` pass. This function performs top-down (caller to callee) propagation of the `nofpclass` attribute. It iterates over all users of a function (which must be callsites), intersects the `nofpclass` masks of the return value and arguments across all callsites, and adds the resulting `nofpclass` attributes to the function's return type and arguments.

Formal Semantic Modeling:
Before the fix: The `FunctionAttrs` pass did not propagate `nofpclass` from callers to callees.
After the fix: The pass propagates `nofpclass` from callers to callees by intersecting the masks.
Conditions for difference: When a function has internal linkage and is called with `nofpclass` attributes on the callsite, the function itself will now receive those attributes.

Assumptions and Preconditions Identified:
1. The function assumes that if a function has no users, it is safe to add `nofpclass(all)` to its return type and arguments.
2. The function assumes that `nofpclass` can be added to any argument or return type, regardless of whether it is a floating-point type or not.

Potential Cases to Break Assumptions:
1. If a function has no users (e.g., it is a dead internal function), the loop over users `for (User *U : F.users())` is skipped. The `RetNoFPClass` and `ArgsNoFPClass` masks remain initialized to `fcAllFlags`. The function then proceeds to add `nofpclass(all)` to the return type and all arguments.
2. If the return type or any argument is not a floating-point type (e.g., `i32`, `void`, pointers), adding `nofpclass` to it will cause a verifier failure: "Attribute 'nofpclass' applied to incompatible type!".
3. Even if the types are floating-point, adding `nofpclass(all)` to a dead function is semantically weird (it means the function takes and returns poison), though it might not crash the verifier.

Test Strategies:
We can create test cases with dead internal functions that have non-FP return types or arguments. ## Your Task for Phase 2

In this phase, you will use the `tests_manager` tool to retrieve existing test cases, apply your proposed mutations, and verify them. You must ensure that **every test case** managed by the `tests_manager` is processed and marked as tested.

Follow this structured workflow:

### Step 1: Retrieve and Prioritize Test Cases
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `list` action to see all available test cases and their current status.
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `get` action to retrieve the full details of an untested test case.
- Due to context length and round limit, you should prioritize test cases that are more relevant to the identified issues.

### Step 2: Understand the Test Case
- Analyze the selected test case to understand its structure, input characteristics, and what it is testing.
- Call the `verify` tool to see how the LLVM IR code in the test case is transformed by the optimization pass. This can help you understand the semantics of the test case and how it relates to the fix.

### Step 3: Apply Mutations
For the selected test case, apply mutations according to your proposed test strategies:
- **Focus**: Ensure the mutations focus on exposing the potential issues identified in Phase 1.
- **Format**: Provide each mutated test case in a separate ```llvm ... ``` code block.
- **Coverage**: **CRITICAL**: You must cover all the proposed test strategies across different test cases to ensure comprehensive testing of potential issues.

### Step 4: Verify and Test
- **Verify Tool**: Use the `verify` tool to submit the generated test cases along with your reasoning. This uses `alive2` to check if the test cases expose any issues with the fix.
- **Difftest Tool**: Use the `difftest` tool to execute the original and optimized LLVM IR (transformed by `opt`) with specific input values to check for execution differences.

### Step 5: Analyze Results and Refine
- If the `verify` or `difftest` fails to find issues, refine the test case only if you believe unexplored potential issues remain. Otherwise, move on to other strategies or test cases. Check **Guidelines for Refining Test Cases** below for suggestions on how to refine test cases based on verification results.

### Step 6: Mark as Tested
- Once you have fully explored and verified a test case, use the `tests_manager` tool with the `mark_tested` action to mark it as completed.
- **CRITICAL**: You must repeat this process until the `tests_manager` confirms that **all** test cases have been tested.

Repeat the above steps for each test case until all test cases in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

You are also allowed to generate tests from scratch or find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools if you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues. Make sure you call `verify` and `difftest` tools to check the validity of these test cases and confirm whether the issues can be exposed by actual execution.

---

## Guidelines for Refining Test Cases

### Important Rule
You can always keep mutating the test case and verifying it until you find an issue or are confident that no issues can be found. However, make sure it is aligned with the potential issues you identified in Phase 1. Meanwhile, avoid stucking on a test case for too long. If you have tried multiple mutations and still cannot find any issues, move on to other test cases or strategies.

### Handling Verification Results
- **Failed-to-prove**:
  1. Try to reduce the test case to a smaller example that still fails. This can help isolate the specific conditions that cause the issue.
  2. Try to call `difftest` to run the test case with specific input value that you think can trigger the issue based on your analysis. This can help check if the issue can be exposed by actual execution even if it cannot be proved by alive2.
- **Alive2 errors**: Try to call `trans` to run opt with the same command arguments and adjust the test case or command arguments to fix.
- **Correct transformation**: Try to first analyze if the test strategy is correct and then decide to refine the test case or continue to generate other test cases.

### Additional Notes
- **Coverage**: If you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues, you can also find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools.
- **Completeness**: Make sure you have at least explored all the test strategies you proposed in Phase 1, and generated multiple test cases if possible.
- **Completion**: You cannot finish Phase 2 until all tests in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

---

## Tools that you can use

- `findN`: Search for files in the LLVM codebase related to the component or optimization pass to understand the context of the fix and find relevant tests.
- `listN`: List files in the LLVM codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `readN`: Read the content of a file in the LLVM codebase to understand existing tests or the fix.
- `grepN`: Search for specific patterns in the codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `tests_manager`: Manage the list of test cases. You can list all tests to see their status, get the details of a specific test, or mark a test as tested. Your goal is to ensure all tests are tested.
- `trans`: Run the `opt` tool with specific arguments to see how the LLVM IR code is transformed by the optimization pass.
- `verify`: Use alive2 to verify if the transformation from original LLVM IR code to optimized LLVM IR code is correct, which can help check the validity of generated test cases in Phase 2.
- `difftest`: Use llubi to perform differential testing on the original and transformed LLVM IR code, which can help check if the generated test cases cannot be proved by alive2.
- `report`: End Phase 2 by submitting the generated test cases and their verification results.

{"thoughts":"CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 1: I must use specific tools when available.
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 2: I am using `trans` to test the transformation.
Wait, if a function has a user, and the user is a callsite, but the callsite is dead?
If `user` is exported, it is a root. It calls `dead_internal_non_fp`.
Then `dead_internal_non_fp` is in the `LazyCallGraph`.
Its users are `user`.
The loop `for (User *U : F.users())` will execute.
`CB` is the callsite in `user`.
`CB->getRetNoFPClass()` returns 0.
`RetNoFPClass &= 0` becomes 0.
So `RetNoFPClass` is 0.
It won't add `nofpclass(all)`.
So this is safe.

Wait, what if the callsite is NOT in the module?
No, `F.users()` only returns users in the module.
What if the user is a callsite, but the callsite is in a dead function?
If the dead function is not in the `LazyCallGraph`, then `dead_internal_non_fp` is also not in the `LazyCallGraph`.
So it won't be visited.

Wait, is there ANY way a function can be in the `LazyCallGraph` but have NO users?
No, because if it has no users, it is not reachable from any root, so it is not in the `LazyCallGraph`.
Wait! What if it is a root, but it has internal linkage?
Can an internal function be a root?
Yes, if its address is taken!
If its address is taken, it is a root.
But if its address is taken, it has a user (the instruction that takes its address).
So `F.users()` is not empty!
And the user is not a `CallBase` (or if it is, `getCalledFunction() != &F`).
So the loop `for (User *U : F.users())` will return `false` early!
```cpp
    auto *CB = dyn_cast<CallBase>(U);
    if (!CB || CB->getCalledFunction() != &F)
      return false;
```
So it will return `false` and NOT add `nofpclass(all)`.

Wait, what if the user is a `CallBase` but it's an indirect call?
If it's an indirect call, `CB->getCalledFunction()` is null.
So `CB->getCalledFunction() != &F` is true.
It returns `false` early.

What if the user is a `CallBase` and it calls `F` directly, but it also passes `F` as an argument?
Then `F` has two uses in the same `CallBase`.
One use is the called operand, the other is an argument.
If `U` is the argument use, `dyn_cast<CallBase>(U)` is true.
`CB->getCalledFunction() == &F` is true.
Wait! If `F` is passed as an argument to `F` itself?
Then `CB->getCalledFunction() == &F` is true.
But `F` is also an argument.
Does it return `false`? No, it continues.
But this means `F` has a user.

Wait, is there ANY case where `F.users()` is empty, but `F` is in the `LazyCallGraph`?
What if `F` was a root, but its users were deleted during the pass pipeline, and `LazyCallGraph` hasn't been updated yet?
`LazyCallGraph` is updated by passes. If a pass deletes all users of `F`, `F` becomes dead.
But `FunctionAttrs` is a CGSCC pass. It doesn't delete users before `addNoFPClassAttrsTopDown`.
Wait, `FunctionAttrs` itself doesn't delete users.
So `F.users()` cannot be empty if `F` is in the `LazyCallGraph` and has internal linkage.
Wait, what if `F` has `linkonce_odr` linkage?
`addNoFPClassAttrsTopDown` is only called if `F.hasInternalLinkage()`.
```cpp
      if (!F.isDeclaration() && F.hasInternalLinkage())
        Worklist.push_back(&F);
```
So `F` MUST have internal linkage.
If `F` has internal linkage, it MUST have users to be in the `LazyCallGraph` (since it can't be a root otherwise).
Wait, is that true?
Let's check `LazyCallGraph::build()`.
Roots are functions that are not `hasLocalLinkage()`, or have their address taken.
If `F` has internal linkage (`hasLocalLinkage()`), it is a root ONLY if its address is taken.
If its address is taken, it has at least one user (the address-taking instruction).
If it is not a root, it must be reachable from a root.
If it is reachable from a root, it must be called by some function.
So it has at least one user (the callsite).
Therefore, `F.users()` is NEVER empty for an internal function in the `LazyCallGraph`!
Is this correct?
Let's test this hypothesis. I will create a module with an internal function that has NO users.
I already did, and `trans` showed it was NOT modified.
So `F.users()` is never empty when `addNoFPClassAttrsTopDown` is called!
This means my "Dead Internal Function" strategies are INVALID because the function will never be visited!","orig_ir":"```llvm
define internal i32 @dead_internal_non_fp(i32 %arg) {
  ret i32 %arg
}

define void @user() {
  call i32 @dead_internal_non_fp(i32 0)
  ret void
}
```","args":"-S -passes='cgscc(function-attrs),rpo-function-attrs'"}

```","thoughts":"CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 1: I must use specific tools when available.
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 2: I am using `trans` to test the transformation.
Wait, the `trans` tool didn't crash!
Why?
Let's check `CB->getCalledFunction()`.
If the callsite has a different function type, does `CB->getCalledFunction()` return `&F`?
In LLVM 15+, opaque pointers are used.
The callsite specifies the function type.
```llvm
  %1 = call nofpclass(nan) float @dead_internal_non_fp()
```
The function type of the callsite is `float ()`.
The function type of `@dead_internal_non_fp` is `i32 (i32)`.
Does `CB->getCalledFunction()` return `@dead_internal_non_fp`?
Yes, because the operand is `@dead_internal_non_fp`.
Wait, if `CB->getCalledFunction() == &F`, then `NumArgs` is `F.arg_size()`, which is 1.
Then `I = 0`.
`CB->getArgOperand(0)` is called.
But the callsite has 0 arguments!
`CB->getArgOperand(0)` asserts `i < arg_size()`.
Why didn't it crash?
Let's check if `CB->getCalledFunction()` returns null if the function type mismatches.
```cpp
  Function *getCalledFunction() const {
    if (auto *F = dyn_cast_or_null<Function>(getCalledOperand()))
      if (F->getValueType() == getFunctionType())
        return F;
    return nullptr;
  }
```
Ah! `getCalledFunction()` returns `nullptr` if the function type mismatches!
So `CB->getCalledFunction() != &F` is true!
It returns `false` early!
So it doesn't crash, and it doesn't add attributes.

Wait, what if the function type MATCHES, but the return type is `i32`, and the callsite has `nofpclass`?
Wait, the verifier forbids `nofpclass` on non-FP types.
So the callsite CANNOT have `nofpclass` if the return type is `i32`.
If the callsite has `nofpclass`, the verifier will reject the IR before the pass even runs!
Let's check if `opt` rejects it."}

  unsigned NumArgs = F.arg_size();

      const APFloat *Cst;

But `F` has a non-FP argument, and the callsite passes a constant float to it?

What if `F` has a non-FP argument, and the callsite passes a non-FP value?

Then `if (ArgNoFPClass == fcNone)` is true, so it `continue`s and doesn't add `nofpclass`.

So `ArgsNoFPClass[I]` will have all bits set EXCEPT `fcPosNormal`.
If there is only one callsite, `ArgsNoFPClass[I]` will be `~fcPosNormal`.
Then it adds `nofpclass(nan inf sub zero ...)` to the argument!
Is this correct?
Yes, because the argument is known to be `1.0`, which is `fcPosNormal`.
So it CANNOT be `nan`, `inf`, `sub`, `zero`, etc.
So adding `nofpclass` for all other classes is CORRECT!

Wait, what if the argument is a vector of floats, and the callsite passes a splat vector of `1.0`?
`m_APFloat` matches, `Cst` is `1.0`.
`ArgsNoFPClass[I] &= ~Cst->classify();`.
It adds `nofpclass` for all other classes.
This is CORRECT!

Wait, what if the argument is an array of floats?
`m_APFloat` does NOT match arrays.
So it falls back to `CB->getParamNoFPClass(I)`.
This is safe.

Wait, what if the argument is a struct containing a float?
`m_APFloat` does NOT match structs.
So it falls back to `CB->getParamNoFPClass(I)`.
This is safe.

Wait, what if the argument is a float, and the callsite passes `undef`?
`m_APFloat` does NOT match `undef`.
So it falls back to `CB->getParamNoFPClass(I)`.
This is safe.

Wait, what if the argument is a float, and the callsite passes `poison`?
`m_APFloat` does NOT match `poison`.
So it falls back to `CB->getParamNoFPClass(I)`.
This is safe.

Wait, what if the argument is a float, and the callsite passes a constant expression?
`m_APFloat` might match if it evaluates to a constant float.
If it does, it's safe.

Wait, what if the argument is a float, and the callsite passes a constant float, but the function is NOT internal?
`addNoFPClassAttrsTopDown` is ONLY called if `F.hasInternalLinkage()`.
So this is safe.

Wait, what if the function has NO arguments?
`NumArgs` is 0.
`ArgsNoFPClass` is empty.
The loop over arguments doesn't execute.
This is safe.

Wait, what if the function returns `void`?
`RetNoFPClass &= CB->getRetNoFPClass();`.
Since it returns `void`, `CB->getRetNoFPClass()` is 0.
`RetNoFPClass` becomes 0.
This is safe.

Wait, what if the function returns a float, and the callsite has `nofpclass(nan)`?
`RetNoFPClass &= CB->getRetNoFPClass();`.
`RetNoFPClass` becomes `nan`.
It adds `nofpclass(nan)` to the return type.
This is CORRECT!

`F.addRetAttr(Attribute::getWithNoFPClass(Ctx, RetNoFPClass));`

Let's check `addRetAttr`.
```cpp

```
If we add `nofpclass(nan)`, does it overwrite `nofpclass(inf)`?
Or does it merge them?
If it overwrites, then the new attribute is `nofpclass(nan)`.
But the function actually guarantees `nofpclass(inf)` (from its body) AND `nofpclass(nan)` (from its callers).
So the correct attribute should be `nofpclass(nan inf)`.
If it overwrites, we LOSE the `nofpclass(inf)` attribute!
Wait, `RetNoFPClass` is initialized to `fcAllFlags`.
Then it is intersected with `CB->getRetNoFPClass()`.
So `RetNoFPClass` ONLY contains the classes that ALL callers guarantee.
It does NOT include the classes that the function itself guarantees!
If the function already has `nofpclass(inf)`, `OldAttr` is `inf`.
`RetNoFPClass` is `nan`.
`OldAttr != RetNoFPClass` is true.
It adds `nofpclass(nan)`.
If `addRetAttr` overwrites, the function now has `nofpclass(nan)` instead of `nofpclass(inf)`.
Wait, does `addRetAttr` overwrite?
Let's check `AttributeList::addAttribute`.
If the attribute already exists, it usually replaces it.
Wait, `nofpclass` is a type attribute or an enum attribute?
It's an enum attribute with a value.
If we add `nofpclass(nan)` to a function that already has `nofpclass(inf)`, does it replace it?

CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 2: I am using `report` to submit the bug.
Bug Triggering Analysis:
The `addNoFPClassAttrsTopDown` function checks if `OldAttr != RetNoFPClass`. If they are different, it adds `RetNoFPClass` to the function and sets `Changed = true`.
However, `RetNoFPClass` is the intersection of the `nofpclass` attributes from all callsites.
If the function already has a `nofpclass` attribute that is a SUPERSET of `RetNoFPClass` (e.g., the function has `nofpclass(nan inf)` but the callsite only has `nofpclass(nan)`), then `OldAttr` (`nan inf`) is different from `RetNoFPClass` (`nan`).
The pass will call `F.addRetAttr(Attribute::getWithNoFPClass(Ctx, RetNoFPClass))`.
Because `addAttribute` merges the attributes, the new attribute will be `nan inf | nan = nan inf`.
So the IR is NOT actually changed!
But the pass sets `Changed = true`!
This means the pass reports that it modified the IR, even though it didn't.
If the pass manager runs this pass in a loop (e.g., CGSCC pass manager), it might loop infinitely or cause unnecessary invalidation of analyses!

Fix Weakness Analysis:
The weakness is that the pass doesn't check if `RetNoFPClass` is a SUBSET of `OldAttr`.
It only checks `OldAttr != RetNoFPClass`.
It should check `(OldAttr | RetNoFPClass) != OldAttr` or `(RetNoFPClass & ~OldAttr) != 0`.
The same issue applies to `ArgNoFPClass`.
If `OldAttr == ArgNoFPClass` is false, it adds the attribute and sets `Changed = true`.
But if `ArgNoFPClass` is a subset of `OldAttr`, adding it does nothing, but `Changed` is set to `true`.
This is a classic "infinite loop in pass manager" bug.","test":["```llvm
